planting.note: Direct-sow or transplant in moist to average, well-drained to slightly moist soil in part shade to full shade; widow's tears naturalizes readily and self-sows to maintain a colony. In zone 9–11 it grows as a perennial from fleshy roots; in zone 7–8 it may die back but resprouts in spring. It tolerates dry shade under trees, a rare quality among flowering plants.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; widow's tears (whitemouth dayflower) is a native perennial of the Gulf Coast, Southeast, and tropical America — one of the most genuinely shade-tolerant flowering perennials for the South. The small but intricate flowers are distinctive: three-petaled, with two large, vivid bright-blue upper petals and one tiny, reduced white lower petal, creating a charming, asymmetric, sky-blue and white pattern that inspired the common name 'dayflower' (each flower lasts only one day). The plant blooms continuously from spring through fall, producing a constant succession of new flowers, and self-sows to maintain naturalized colonies in the shade garden. It tolerates dry shade under large trees — a critical landscape niche where few flowering plants will grow. The foliage is attractive and grass-like, with lance-shaped, pleated leaves. In zone 9–11 it grows from fleshy roots and persists as a perennial. An excellent, low-maintenance flowering groundcover for the shaded edges of Gulf Coast gardens.

subcategory: native prairie and woodland edge perennial

planting.note: Direct-sow in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flowering spurge develops a deep taproot and resists transplanting once established — site carefully. It thrives in lean, dry soils and drought conditions and will self-sow to establish a colony over time. Wear gloves when handling as the milky sap is a skin and eye irritant.

directions: Sow in lean, well-drained, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flowering spurge is a graceful, airy native perennial found in prairies, roadsides, and open woodlands from Ontario to Florida, producing clouds of tiny white flowers with bright-white petal-like bracts from June through September — the entire upper portion of the plant becomes a frothy white mist of bloom in summer. Its structural similarity to Baby's Breath (Gypsophila) in the garden is striking — the branching sprays of tiny white flowers have the same airy, cloud-like quality and fill a similar garden role, but as a long-lived native perennial adapted to dry, lean soils. The milky sap is caustic and was used medicinally by Native Americans. Flowering spurge is drought-tolerant, self-sowing, and an important late-season nectar source for butterflies including skippers, hairstreaks, and sulphurs that congregate on the flowers. The gray-green foliage turns reddish in fall. An outstanding filler plant for lean, dry soils in full sun.

planting.note: Direct-sow scarified seeds in lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; goat's rue grows naturally in dry, sandy soils of pine barrens, open woodlands, and roadsides and thrives in lean conditions where most ornamentals fail. It is a nitrogen-fixer that improves soil fertility over time. The deep, rope-like roots (giving rise to 'Devil's Shoestring') make established plants drought-tolerant but resistant to transplanting.

directions: Direct-sow in lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; goat's rue is a low, spreading native legume with bicolored flowers in a striking combination — cream-white wings contrasting with a magenta-pink standard (the upper petal), creating a two-toned floral effect in clusters at the stem tips from May through July. The silky, pinnate leaves with soft, silvery hairs are attractive throughout the season. As a legume, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen and naturally improves poor, sandy soils over time — a characteristic adaptation to the nutrient-poor pine barrens and sandhill habitats where it grows. The deep, twisted, rope-like roots (hence 'Devil's Shoestring') make mature plants drought-tolerant but difficult to transplant. It is the sole larval host plant for the Frosted Elfin butterfly (Callophrys irus), a declining species that depends entirely on goat's rue for caterpillar food. An outstanding choice for naturalistic dry gardens, sandy soils, and butterfly conservation plantings.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; roundhead bush clover fixes nitrogen and thrives in poor, infertile soil — avoid rich, moist conditions which produce excessive, floppy growth. It develops a deep taproot and is difficult to transplant once established; site carefully.

directions: Direct-sow scarified seeds in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; roundhead bush clover is a tall, upright native prairie legume producing dense, rounded, button-like flower heads of small white pea flowers with purple markings in July and August — the spherical, dense heads clustered at intervals along stiff, silver-hairy stems giving the plant its common name. The deeply divided, trifoliate, silver-silky leaves are attractive all season. After flowering, the heads develop into dense, silvery-tan, persistent seed clusters that remain on the upright stems through winter, providing both ornamental structure in the winter landscape and critically important food for wildlife: the seeds are eaten by quail, turkey, pheasants, songbirds, and small mammals and are considered one of the highest-value native wildlife food plants of the eastern prairie. A nitrogen-fixer adapted to dry, lean, infertile soils where the usual prairie companions (big bluestem, coneflowers, black-eyed Susan) also thrive.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline limestone-based soil in full sun; Engelmann's sage is endemic to the limestone hills of the Edwards Plateau in central Texas and naturally adapted to alkaline, rocky, shallow soils with excellent drainage. It performs poorly in heavy, moist, or acidic soils.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Engelmann's sage is a rare, endemic Texas Hill Country salvia found naturally only on the limestone hills and canyons of the Edwards Plateau in central Texas. It produces dense spikes of vivid violet-blue to blue-purple flowers with white markings from spring through fall, blooming particularly heavily after rains — a characteristic adaptation to seasonal rainfall patterns. The plant forms a compact, drought-hardy subshrub with small, gray-green, aromatic, woolly-textured leaves that are pleasant-scented when brushed. More compact and refined in habit than many salvias and exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established in well-drained soil. It is not widely known outside Texas native plant circles but is an outstanding, drought-tolerant perennial for zone 7–10 gardens with alkaline soil — a category that includes much of the Texas Hill Country, where it can be grown as a true perennial.
